footer.brandDescription: Open-source infrastructure for sovereign AI discovery. The registry points; the provider operates; the hosting environment secures.

hero.subtitle: Govern, orchestrate, and monitor trusted AI agents, models, and MCP infrastructure from a unified sovereign platform - built for nations, regulators, and the enterprises they depend on.

registrySection.heading: Discover what {jurisdiction} can <accent>trust and integrate</accent>.
registrySection.description: The registry points to locally-relevant AI resources - never hosts them. Each listing carries a verifiable provider, a sovereignty review, and a stable AIR-ID under <mono>air://</mono>.
registrySection.showingCount: Showing {count} of {total} public {total, plural, one {listing} other {listings}}.
registrySection.listPageSubtitle: Browse public listings and filter by kind. Listings carry verifiable providers and stable AIR-IDs.

providersSection.crumb: Providers · The organisations behind the registry
providersSection.heading: Meet the organisations <accent>{jurisdiction} already trusts</accent>.
providersSection.subtitle: Browse the sovereign operators, model labs, hosting partners and accredited integrators behind every listing in the registry. Each provider carries a verifiable status and a public profile.
providersSection.description: Browse the sovereign operators, model labs, hosting partners and accredited integrators behind every listing in the registry. Each provider carries a verifiable status and a public profile.
providersSection.showingCount: Showing {count} of {total} public {total, plural, one {provider} other {providers}}.

governance.heading: Listing is not endorsement.
governance.headingAccent: Three independent signals do the work.
governance.description: The registry exposes lightweight governance metadata so users can tell apart "this resource exists" from "an authorised body has officially endorsed it." Status labels are explicit and auditable.
governance.providerVerificationDesc: Domain-bound DNS and email proofs confirm that listed providers are who they claim to be.
governance.sovereigntyReviewDesc: Each resource cites local law, data, systems, language or culture - and is reviewed against a published rubric.
governance.runtimeIdentityDesc: Optional SPIFFE/SPIRE SVIDs from the hosting environment - issued by the operator, never the registry.
governance.openAuditLogDesc: Status changes, reviewer notes and AIR-ID assignments are signed and publicly auditable.
governance.sovereigntyTestDesc: To qualify, a submission must cite at least one sovereignty basis with concrete evidence - a referenced law, dataset, institution, language asset or cultural artefact.
governance.localLawDesc: Encodes legislation, regulation, official process or professional obligation.
governance.localDataDesc: Uses local datasets, records or locally collected knowledge.
governance.localSystemsDesc: Integrates with or describes local institutional systems and workflows.
governance.languageCultureDesc: Supports local language, culture, norms or context.
governance.qualityNote: Quality matters more than quantity. A registry of fifty credible resources is more useful than one of a thousand generic listings.

governancePage.pageSubtitle: The discipline of the registry is in what it refuses to do. Many adjacent capabilities are reasonable; each one would make the registry a different kind of platform.
governancePage.whatItIsItem1: A locally-governed catalogue of sovereign AI resources.
governancePage.whatItIsItem3: Three independent governance signals: provider-verified, sovereignty-reviewed, official-resource.
governancePage.whatItIsNotItem4: A hosting provider for any AI resource.
governancePage.reviewBoardBody1: Reviewers apply the published <link>sovereignty rubric</link> to elevate or reject submissions. The board is composed of named reviewers drawn from the operator, sector experts and (where applicable) official authorities. Decisions are public; reviewer notes appear on the resource detail page; every decision is timestamped against the rubric version applied at the time.
governancePage.reviewBoardBody2: Reviewers do not act on resources from their own organisation. Conflicts of interest are recorded under <link>disclosure</link>.
governancePage.appealsIntro: If a resource was rejected, deprecated or removed and the provider believes the decision was wrong, the appeal path is:
governancePage.appealsStep1: Submit an appeal through <link>the contact form</link> with the AIR-ID and a short statement of why the original decision should be re-examined.
governancePage.appealsStep2: The operator routes the appeal to a reviewer who was not involved in the original decision.
governancePage.appealsStep3: The reviewer publishes a re-examination outcome with reasoning. The outcome is appended to the audit log alongside the original decision.
governancePage.appealsNote: Appeals are not a route to bypass the <link>sovereignty rubric</link>; they are a route to test that it was applied correctly.
governancePage.disclosureIntro: The operator ({operatorName} for {portalDomain}) commits to disclose:
governancePage.disclosureItem1: Funding and operating sponsors of the registry instance.
governancePage.disclosureItem2: Any resources or providers in which the operator has an ownership or hosting relationship.
governancePage.disclosureItem3: Reviewer roster and any standing conflicts of interest declared by individual reviewers.
governancePage.disclosureItem4: Material changes to the rubric, schema or APIs.
governancePage.disclosureNote: All disclosure events are recorded in the <link>audit log</link>.
governancePage.publicLogBody: Every state-changing action - AIR-ID issuance, sovereignty review, official-resource endorsement, lifecycle transition - is recorded in an append-only <link>audit log</link>. The log behind a removed resource is preserved indefinitely so the governance trail remains traceable.

registryDetail.deprecatedDesc: this resource is still publicly listed for continuity, but its provider has marked it for retirement. New integrations should look for a replacement.
registryDetail.airIdNotIssued: AIR-ID not yet issued (resource has not reached listed).
registryDetail.noSovereigntyEvidence: No public sovereignty evidence has been attached yet.
registryDetail.reportTitle: See something off about this listing?
registryDetail.reportDesc: Flag impersonation, broken endpoints, missing sovereignty evidence, or anything else that doesn't match the registry's rules. The operator reviews every report.
registryDetail.footerDisclaimer: Listing is not endorsement. The registry points; the provider operates; the hosting environment secures. Sovereignty review and official-resource authorisation are independent governance signals - see the <link>governance charter</link>.

providerDetail.noPublicResources: This provider has no public resources in the registry yet.
providerDetail.footerDisclaimer: Listing is not endorsement. The registry points; the provider operates; the hosting environment secures. Sovereignty review and official-resource authorisation are independent governance signals - see the <link>governance charter</link>.

dashboard.title: Sovereign control plane
dashboard.subtitle: Operator console for governance, lifecycle, and audit. All queues route through this portal.
dashboard.sovereigntyReviewQueueBody: Open reviews for submitted resources - apply the §11 checklist, approve, reject, or request changes.
dashboard.auditLogBody: Append-only governance ledger. Every status transition, role change, and listing decision lands here.
dashboard.resourcesBody: Catalogue management - search, filter, and lifecycle-action every listed resource across all providers.
dashboard.providersBody: Verify provider identity, suspend, or elevate to official-provider posture.
dashboard.openComplaintsAssigned: {count} open {count, plural, one {complaint} other {complaints}} assigned to you
dashboard.complaintsWaiting: Hi {name}, these are waiting on you. Tap to open the filtered queue.

resources.title: Resources
resources.subtitle: Models, agents, tools and skills across every publisher. Add new creates a draft on behalf of an existing provider; lifecycle transitions live on the per-row action menu.

users.title: Users & roles
users.subtitle: Operators across all four portals. Add new sends a verification / invite email; the recipient sets their password via the standard reset flow.

audit.title: Audit log
audit.subtitle: Append-only ledger of governance-relevant actions. Latest 200 entries shown.

settings.title: Settings
settings.subtitle: Deployment configuration. These values are sourced from config at boot - change them in .env then redeploy. Reference taxonomies are editable through the Reference Tables surface.

complaints.title: Complaints
complaints.subtitle: Public complaints filed against providers or resources.

policies.title: Policies
policies.subtitle: Governance policies published by the registry operator.
policies.ruleListingNotEndorsement: Listing ≠ endorsement. Public detail pages and footer copy carry the disclaimer; copy may not be removed by deployments.
policies.ruleSeparationOfDuties: Separation of duties (§11). Reviewers and admins cannot decide a review against their own provider record. Enforced by assertCanReview() in src/lib/auth/separation-of-duties.ts.
policies.ruleAppendOnlyAudit: Append-only audit (§18). Every governance mutation writes one AuditLog row through writeAudit(). Retention ≥ 24 months.
policies.rulePublicSafe: Public-safe projections. Internal review notes, complainant PII, and reset/verification tokens never cross src/lib/discovery/serializers.ts.
policies.providerVerificationNote: Operators set provider status via POST /api/admin/providers/[id]/verify (T035) — every change writes a paired provider_verification trust signal.
policies.sovereigntyChecklistNote: Reviewers must record an answer (yes / no / n_a) for every item before approving a review; any no answer blocks approval. Wired into POST /api/admin/reviews/[id]/decide.

contacts.title: Contact messages
contacts.subtitle: Messages received through the public contact form.
contacts.emptyState: No contact messages match this filter.

providers.title: Providers
providers.subtitle: Provider organisations registered in the registry.

integrations.title: Integrations
integrations.subtitle: External service connections and API integrations.
integrations.smtpDetailUnconfigured: No SMTP host set – verification, password reset, and contact replies log to console (dev mode). From: {from}
integrations.mcpDetail: Tools: registry.list / registry.get / registry.resolve / registry.discover / registry.well_known. Streamable HTTP is implemented via JSON-RPC 2.0 over POST.
integrations.openapiDetail: Self-describing surface for the public REST + authenticated subsets. Mirrors module-level api.yaml files.
integrations.capabilityDetail: Identity domain, supported types/languages, endpoint templates. Auto-discovered by foreign integrators.
integrations.healthDetail: DB-readiness probe used by load balancers + the smoke runner.
integrations.oidcDetailUnconfigured: Reserved as a config-driven extension. The schema's Account table is ready; wiring lands when OIDC_* env vars are populated.
integrations.federationDetail: Cross-registry mirror is schema-only in MVP – Resource.sourceRegistryId / sourceAirId are populated via worker once federation lands (post-Phase 5).
integrations.observabilityDetail: Audit log + email-attempt logs are stdout-only in v0.4. Wiring to a hosted sink (e.g. OpenTelemetry, Loki) is operator-driven post-Phase 5.

branding.title: Branding
branding.subtitle: Customise the public-facing appearance of the registry portal.

site.subtitle: Manage the public portal's marketing content.
site.fallbackNote: Each section falls back to a hardcoded default if the corresponding table is empty, so a fresh deploy renders correctly out of the box before any rows exist. Run pnpm db:seed once on bootstrap to populate the tables with the legacy hardcoded strings; subsequent seeds will not overwrite admin edits.

docs.title: Documentation
docs.subtitle: Technical documentation for operators, providers, and integrators.
docs.pageSubtitle: Everything you need to publish, resolve and audit listings against the v0.4 reference implementation at {portalDomain}.
docs.section_overview_body: AIR-SPEC 0.4 defines the listing schema, sovereignty rubric, identifier format, and verification proofs the registry implements.
docs.section_metadata_body: Provider, kind, sovereignty bases with evidence, contact, terms, license, region, optional SPIFFE trust domain.
docs.section_verification_body: DNS TXT and email-based proofs. Any mismatch flips status to "unverified" and surfaces a public note.
docs.section_review_body: Reviewers apply the published checklist, record signed notes, and assign a status. Appeals are public.

ecosystem.title: Ecosystem
ecosystem.subtitle: The broader AI ecosystem connected to the registry.
ecosystem.pageSubtitle: The AI Registry, the operators who run it, the integrators who connect it, the audiences it serves, and the path to federation - independent parties held together only by open standards and stable identifiers.
ecosystem.platformDescription: The registry catalogues sovereign AI resources — models, agents, and MCP tools — and exposes them through a stable discovery API. It never sits on the runtime path.
ecosystem.integratorsDescription: Implementation partners, system integrators and independent reviewers who participate in sovereign deployments and governance reviews.
ecosystem.audiencesDescription: Decision-makers, regulators, developers, researchers, and citizens — everyone who needs trustworthy, discoverable AI infrastructure.
ecosystem.federationDescription: The architecture is designed for federation. Each sovereign jurisdiction can run its own registry instance while remaining interoperable through shared AIR-IDs and open standards.
ecosystem.ctaDescription: Whether you're a government body, a model provider, a hosting partner or an integrator — the registry is open for participation.
ecosystem.layer1Desc: Runs the public portal, discovery API and review workflow. Issues AIR-IDs and maintains the audit log.
ecosystem.layer2Desc: Owns the resource: endpoints, documentation, terms of access, version control. Remains fully responsible.
ecosystem.layer3Desc: Runs the actual workload - sovereign cloud, GPU, on-prem - and may issue runtime SVIDs via SPIFFE/SPIRE.
ecosystem.operatorsDescription: Best operated by organisations that already run trusted, neutral, national-scale digital services on behalf of broader ecosystems - the legitimacy, capability and convening power matter as much as the technology. {operatorName} operates the reference instance at {portalDomain}.
ecosystem.dpiEnablerPoint1: Trusted to operate critical digital infrastructure for many parties, not just for themselves.
ecosystem.dpiEnablerPoint2: Already integrated with government, industry and developer ecosystems.
ecosystem.dpiEnablerPoint3: Comfortable with standards, interconnection and long-lived public services.
ecosystem.dpiEnablerPoint4: Predictable governance and operational continuity over years, not project cycles.
ecosystem.operatorProfilesDesc: In any given country, one or two organisations typically stand out.
ecosystem.telcosDesc: National infrastructure, enterprise ecosystems, interconnection, deep government relationships, hosting adjacency and sovereign-cloud ambitions - though hosting AI remains separate from operating the registry.
ecosystem.governmentDesc: The most resilient model is a partnership: government provides policy legitimacy and sector convening; the operator (telco or other DPI enabler) provides platform operations and technical implementation.
ecosystem.integratorRole1Desc: Help providers prepare submissions, gather sovereignty evidence and integrate AIR-IDs into existing systems.
ecosystem.integratorRole2Desc: Supply reviewer capacity, subject-matter expertise and second-opinion checks for the sovereignty rubric.
ecosystem.integratorRole3Desc: Extend AIR-Core for a jurisdiction - schema additions, sector configuration, federation tooling.
ecosystem.integratorRole4Desc: Train provider teams, run discovery workshops and embed the registry into national AI strategies.
ecosystem.audience1Point1: A sovereign locus for AI discovery, distinct from global platforms and free of vendor lock-in.
ecosystem.audience1Point2: Practical instrument for national AI strategy: visible capabilities, named providers, transparent review.
ecosystem.audience1Point3: Foundation for DPI that complements identity, payments and data-exchange platforms.
ecosystem.audience2Point1: A credible place to publish locally relevant AI resources to a known audience.
ecosystem.audience2Point2: Provider verification and official-resource status - signals trust without legal exposure.
ecosystem.audience2Point3: Standardised metadata that lets resources plug into AI systems, agents and integrations.
ecosystem.audience3Point1: Single, machine-readable place to discover sovereign AI by jurisdiction, capability or sovereignty basis.
ecosystem.audience3Point2: Stable identifiers (AIR-IDs) that survive provider rebrands or endpoint changes.
ecosystem.audience3Point3: Clear endpoint and protocol metadata: REST today, MCP and A2A as ecosystems mature.
ecosystem.audience4Point1: AI systems that can find and use locally accurate tools - tax calculators, legal references, services.
ecosystem.audience4Point2: Visible governance signals that distinguish 'officially endorsed' from 'self-declared'.
ecosystem.audience4Point3: A sovereign alternative to opaque global directories where local relevance is invisible.
ecosystem.federationPrinciple1Desc: Registries federate by deliberate agreement; no automatic or transitive trust.
ecosystem.federationPrinciple2Desc: Discovery references - not shared databases or runtime access.
ecosystem.federationPrinciple3Desc: Hosting providers may bind workloads to AIR-IDs via SPIFFE/SPIRE - outside registry scope.
ecosystem.federationPrinciple4Desc: Each registry remains independently operated, governed and populated.
ecosystem.engageDescription: Intentionally small, open and sovereign. The value comes from restraint - and from others deploying and governing their own. {operatorName} is building the reference; the next steps are adoption, contribution and partnership.
ecosystem.track1Desc: Stand up a national or municipal AI Registry using the open-source AIR-Core platform with your own configuration.
ecosystem.track2Desc: Improve the open-source codebase, propose schema changes, share country-specific configuration or sovereignty rubrics.
ecosystem.track3Desc: Co-publish governance models, federation agreements, or shared reviewer rosters with peer registries.
ecosystem.track4Desc: Track {portalDomain}, study the operating model, and decide if and how to deploy in your context.

pricing.title: Pricing
pricing.subtitle: The registry is free and open-source.
pricing.pageSubtitle: The registry is digital public infrastructure. Listing, browsing, search and AIR-ID resolution are free for providers, developers, citizens and AI systems. There are no listing fees, no commission, and no paid tiers.
pricing.whatIsFreeItem1: Submitting a resource to the registry.
pricing.whatIsFreeItem2: Browsing, searching and reading public listings.
pricing.whatIsFreeItem3: Getting an AIR-ID issued when a resource is elevated past draft.
pricing.whatIsFreeItem4: Calling the public discovery API and the AIR-ID resolver.
pricing.whatIsFreeItem5: Standing up your own registry from the open-source AIR-Core platform.
pricing.whatRegistryDoesNotDoTitle: What the registry does not do
pricing.whatRegistryDoesNotDoBody: The registry is not a marketplace, billing platform or payment processor. It does not take a commission on transactions, host AI workloads, or charge providers for verification or endorsement. Commercial agreements between consumers and providers happen directly between those parties - outside the registry.
pricing.operatorCostsBody: Operating the registry is funded by its operator ({operatorName} for {portalDomain}) as part of its digital public infrastructure mandate. Other jurisdictions stand up their own instance, configured for their identity domain and reviewer pool, with operating costs absorbed by their own DPI enabler.

terms.title: Terms of Use
terms.pageSubtitle: The plain-language terms that govern the use of the {registryName} public portal and APIs.
terms.registryPointsTitle: The registry points; the provider operates
terms.registryPointsBody: {registryName} is a discovery layer. It lists, describes and resolves locally relevant AI resources. It does not host, gateway, proxy, authenticate or rate-limit calls to the resources it lists. When you discover a resource here, you go directly to the provider; the provider's own terms apply to your actual use of the resource.
terms.listingNotEndorsementBody1: A public listing means a resource has been submitted and (where applicable) reviewed. It is not a certification, a recommendation, or a guarantee of fitness for any purpose. Read the
terms.listingNotEndorsementBody2: on each listing to understand what trust is on offer.
terms.acceptableUseBody1: Use of this portal and its APIs is subject to the
terms.acceptableUseBody2: . In short: do not abuse the API; do not impersonate providers; do not submit resources you do not have the right to publish.
terms.noWarrantyBody: The portal and APIs are provided on an "as is" basis. The operator ({operatorName}) makes no warranty about the accuracy or completeness of third-party metadata, and is not responsible for the resources providers point to. Liability for any resource rests with its provider.
terms.changesBody1: These terms may evolve as the registry matures. Material changes are recorded in the

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.pageSubtitle: What data the registry holds, what it does with it, and what rights you have under data protection law.
privacy.whatWeHoldIntro: The registry holds two kinds of data:
privacy.whatWeHoldItem1: Public listing metadata - providers, resources, governance signals, sovereignty claims, endpoints. This is intentionally public and available through the discovery API.
privacy.whatWeHoldItem2: Account data - name, email, organisation and role for registered providers, reviewers and operators. Used only to authenticate, route review work and contact you about the registry.
privacy.lawfulBasisBody: Account data is held under the {privacyDataProtectionAct}. The lawful basis is either contract performance (we cannot run your provider account without it) or legitimate interest (operating a public registry of national interest).
privacy.whatWeDontDoBody: We do not sell account data. We do not share it with third parties for marketing. We do not host or proxy calls to the AI resources we list, so we do not see end-user prompts or responses - those go directly between consumer and provider.
privacy.yourRightsBody1: You can request access to, correction of, or deletion of your account data at any time through
privacy.yourRightsBody2: . If your resource has been publicly listed, the AIR-ID and the underlying audit log persist (append-only) - the resource record can be tombstoned, but the governance trail behind a previously public listing is itself a public record.
privacy.operatorBody: {registryName} is operated by {operatorName} in collaboration with the Ministry of Information.

acceptableUse.title: Acceptable Use Policy
acceptableUse.pageSubtitle: A short, plain-language policy on how to use the public portal, the discovery API and the submission flow.
acceptableUse.whenSubmitItem1: You must be authorised to publish the resource on behalf of the provider.
acceptableUse.whenSubmitItem2: Sovereignty claims must be backed by genuine evidence. False claims will be rejected and may lead to a provider account being suspended.
acceptableUse.whenSubmitItem3: Do not impersonate another provider or use a name or domain you don't control.
acceptableUse.whenSubmitItem4: Provider verification and official-resource endorsement require explicit authorisation from the relevant party.
acceptableUse.whenCallApisTitle: When you call the public APIs
acceptableUse.whenCallApisItem1: Be a good citizen of the public API: use pagination, cache responses, identify your client in the User-Agent header.
acceptableUse.whenCallApisItem2: Do not scrape aggressively or attempt denial-of-service.
acceptableUse.whenCallApisItem3: If you redistribute registry metadata, attribute back to the operating instance (e.g. {portalDomain}).
acceptableUse.willNotListTitle: What the registry will not list
acceptableUse.willNotListItem1: Resources whose primary purpose is unlawful or harmful.
acceptableUse.willNotListItem2: Resources that require runtime data the provider has no lawful basis to use.
acceptableUse.willNotListItem3: Resources that misrepresent provider identity or official endorsement.
acceptableUse.reportingAppealsBody1: To report a listing that you believe breaches this policy, use the
acceptableUse.reportingAppealsBody2: . If your resource was rejected or removed and you believe the decision was wrong, see the

whitepaper.title: Whitepaper
whitepaper.pageSubtitle: A registry-only pattern for discovering locally relevant AI resources while leaving hosting, access, runtime identity and liability with the rightful providers.
whitepaper.whyNowBody1: AI is moving from chat interfaces to connected systems. Models, agents, tools and skills that understand local law, language, data and institutions are becoming part of national digital infrastructure - but they are scattered, hard to find, and invisible to the AI systems that should use them.
whitepaper.whyNowBody2: Sovereign AI matters more, not less. The most useful AI for citizens, businesses and public agencies is rarely the largest global model. It is the model that knows the country: a Kreol language model, a Mauritius tax skill, an agent that helps with company registration, a tool that exposes a public service.
whitepaper.whyNowBody3: Without a sovereign discovery layer, useful local resources stay invisible. Countries risk building disconnected AI assets instead of an AI ecosystem.
whitepaper.disciplineTitle: The discipline: what it is, what it is not
whitepaper.disciplineBody1: The registry separates three concerns that other platforms collapse: discovery, provider operations, and hosting. Each is operated by a different party. The registry is only the first layer.
whitepaper.disciplineBody2: The registry points. The provider operates. The hosting environment secures. That boundary is what makes the registry trustworthy at national scale.
whitepaper.disciplineBody3: It is not an AI hosting platform, gateway, runtime, access-control layer, marketplace, billing platform, legal certifier, or workload-identity issuer. Listing is not endorsement.
whitepaper.whatGetsListedBody1: Four resource types - model, agent, tool, skill - and only resources that meet a sovereignty test: at least one of local law, local data, local systems, or local language and culture, backed by concrete evidence.
whitepaper.whatGetsListedBody2: Quality matters more than quantity. A registry of fifty credible, well-described resources is more useful than one of a thousand generic listings.
whitepaper.governanceBody: Every public resource carries <link>three independent governance signals</link>: provider verification, sovereignty review, and official-resource status. Keeping them separate prevents the most common failure of public registries: becoming a de-facto certifier and attracting liability that was never intended.
whitepaper.companionDocsBody: This whitepaper sits alongside the <docsLink>AIR-SPEC 0.4 technical specification</docsLink> and the <govLink>governance charter</govLink>.

openData.title: Open Data
openData.pageSubtitle: Registry metadata is public by design. Browse, export, mirror or federate - with attribution.
openData.whatIsOpenBody: Every public listing, provider profile, governance signal and AIR-ID is available through the discovery API without authentication. The audit log is append-only and queryable. Nothing in the public layer is paywalled.
openData.airIdsPermanentBody: Once issued, an AIR-ID is a stable identifier. Metadata versions may change, but the identifier itself does not. Tombstones explain removals; they do not erase history.
openData.immutableAuditBody: Governance actions - reviews, appeals, lifecycle transitions - are written to an append-only audit log. Entries are immutable. They survive provider rebrands, endpoint changes and version bumps. If a resource is removed, the AIR-ID resolves to a tombstone explaining why - never silently disappears.
openData.licensingBody: Registry metadata is open. Reuse, mirror, federate or redistribute it freely; attribute back to the operating instance (e.g. {portalDomain}) when republishing. The reference implementation is licensed
openData.seeAlsoBody2: for API endpoints and export formats, or browse the

verification.title: Verification
verification.pageSubtitle: Every public resource carries three orthogonal signals. They are displayed as independent badges. They are never combined into a single tier number - that conflation is the most common failure mode of public registries.
verification.providerVerificationQuestion: is this provider really who they say they are?
verification.providerVerificationNote: Provider verification confirms identity. It does not certify every resource the provider submits.
verification.sovereigntyReviewQuestion: does this resource actually encode something locally relevant?
verification.sovereigntyReviewNote: Reviewers apply the published <link>sovereignty rubric</link> and record their reasoning. Decisions are public.
verification.officialResourceQuestion: has the official provider explicitly authorised this resource?
verification.officialResourceNote: Official-resource status is a stronger endorsement: it requires explicit authorisation by the official provider, not merely provider verification.
verification.whyThreeBody: Collapsing these into a single tier number turns the registry into a de-facto certifier and attracts liability that was never intended. Three independent signals keep responsibility where it belongs: the provider operates and remains liable; the registry exposes lightweight signals so users can tell "this resource exists" from "an authorised body has officially endorsed this resource."

sovereigntyRubric.title: Sovereignty Rubric
sovereigntyRubric.pageSubtitle: To be elevated past draft, a resource must cite at least one sovereignty basis with concrete evidence. The aim is to keep 'sovereign' specific, not aspirational.
sovereigntyRubric.lawMeaning: Encodes local legislation, regulation, official process or professional obligation.
sovereigntyRubric.lawEvidence: Citation of statute (e.g. Data Protection Act 2017), regulator reference, professional body mandate.
sovereigntyRubric.dataMeaning: Uses local datasets, records or locally collected knowledge.
sovereigntyRubric.dataEvidence: Dataset name and provenance, source institution, collection methodology summary.
sovereigntyRubric.systemsMeaning: Integrates with or describes local institutional systems and workflows.
sovereigntyRubric.systemsEvidence: Named system (e.g. CBRD, MNS, MRA), integration contract or documented workflow.
sovereigntyRubric.languageCultureMeaning: Supports local language, culture, norms or context.
sovereigntyRubric.languageCultureEvidence: Language asset (corpus, lexicon), cultural artefact reference, BCP-47 code (e.g. mfe).
sovereigntyRubric.howClaimReviewedBody: For each claim, the reviewer applies a published checklist and records the decision (PENDING · ACCEPTED · REJECTED), plus public reviewer notes. Every claim is timestamped against the rubric version applied at review time, so historical decisions remain interpretable when the rubric is later updated.
sovereigntyRubric.whereFitsBody: Sovereignty review is one of three independent governance signals. See <verificationLink>verification proofs</verificationLink> for the full picture, and <docsLink>AIR-SPEC 0.4 §4</docsLink> for the normative form.

publicAudit.pageSubtitle: Every state-changing action against the registry is recorded. The audit trail is the operator's public commitment to traceable, reversible governance.
publicAudit.whatIsRecordedItem1: AIR-ID issuance when a resource is elevated past draft.
publicAudit.whatIsRecordedItem2: Sovereignty review decisions (basis, status, reviewer notes, rubric version).
publicAudit.whatIsRecordedItem6: Schema or configuration changes affecting public listings.
publicAudit.appendOnlyBody: Audit rows are never deleted or rewritten. Resources are soft-deleted via a deletedAt field, but the audit trail behind a removed resource stays intact and resolvable. Reviewer notes on sovereignty and official-resource decisions are public on the resource detail page.
publicAudit.publicLogBody: A read-only public log of recent events will appear here. While the listing surface is being built out, the underlying audit data is already captured for every governance action. See <docsLink>AIR-SPEC 0.4 §8</docsLink> for the normative form, and <govLink>governance</govLink> for who decides what.

This permanently removes the entry from

adminLifecycle.noTransitions: No lifecycle transitions available from this status.
adminLifecycle.actionHint_approve: Marks the resource as listed and mints the AIR-ID.
adminLifecycle.actionHint_reject: Sends the resource back to needs_update.
adminLifecycle.actionHint_suspend: Hides the resource from the public registry while keeping it in the catalogue.
adminLifecycle.actionHint_restore: Reverts the resource to listed from any status (including suspended, deprecated, removed, or a pre-approval state). Mints the AIR-ID if one was never issued.
adminLifecycle.actionHint_deprecate: Marks the resource as deprecated while keeping the public detail visible.
adminLifecycle.actionHint_remove: Tombstones the resource. The public detail returns 410 Gone but the AIR-ID stays reserved.
adminLifecycle.notifyByEmail: Email the provider's contacts about this transition

reportModal.title: Report this listing
reportModal.receivedBody: Thanks. The review board will examine this report and respond within 5 working days.
reportModal.detailsPlaceholder: What's wrong, and how do you know? Links to evidence are welcome.
reportModal.errorDetailsMin: Add at least 12 characters of context
reportModal.errorSubmitFailed: Could not submit your report. Please retry.

whatGetsListed.heading: Three resource types.
whatGetsListed.description: The registry catalogues sovereign AI resources — models, agents, and skills — each carrying a stable AIR-ID and verifiable metadata.
whatGetsListed.modelDesc: Large language models, vision models, and locally fine-tuned variants hosted inside sovereign infrastructure.
whatGetsListed.agentDesc: Task-oriented agents that combine models, tools, and business logic to execute multi-step workflows.
whatGetsListed.skillDesc: Discrete capabilities — API wrappers, data connectors, compliance checks — callable by agents or directly.

listingCriteria.headingAccent: Every listing must prove at least one sovereignty basis.
listingCriteria.description: To qualify, a submission must cite at least one sovereignty basis with concrete evidence — a referenced law, dataset, institution, language asset or cultural artefact.
listingCriteria.howItWorksDesc: Each submission names one or more bases and supplies <highlight>concrete evidence</highlight> — a referenced law, a dataset URI, an institutional integration, or a language/cultural asset. Reviewers verify the claim against a published rubric; the result is recorded in the audit log.
listingCriteria.qualityBody: A registry of fifty credible resources is more useful than one of a thousand generic listings.
listingCriteria.base1Desc: Encodes local legislation, regulation, official process or professional obligation.
listingCriteria.base2Desc: Uses local datasets, records or locally collected knowledge.
listingCriteria.base3Desc: Integrates with or describes local institutional systems and workflows.
listingCriteria.base4Desc: Supports local language, culture, norms or context.

howItWorks.heading: From submission to use.
howItWorks.step1Desc: Provider submits the resource with metadata and sovereignty evidence.
howItWorks.step2Desc: Reviewer applies the sovereignty rubric and records reviewer notes.
howItWorks.step3Desc: Operator publishes the listing and issues the stable AIR-ID.
howItWorks.step4Desc: Consumer finds the resource through the portal or discovery API.
howItWorks.step5Desc: Consumer calls the provider directly - runtime never touches the registry.
howItWorks.step6Desc: Provider keeps metadata accurate; status reflects any changes over time.

promo.fallbackBody: You keep hosting, access, runtime identity, and liability. The registry just helps the right people find you. Submission is open and reviewed within 10 working days.

orchestration.heading: A resource's journey through
orchestration.description: From initial submission to ongoing maintenance, each stage is logged, auditable, and governed by clear policy.
orchestration.stage1Desc: Provider submits the resource with metadata, sovereignty evidence, and hosting details.
orchestration.stage2Desc: Automated checks confirm provider identity via DNS and email proofs.
orchestration.stage3Desc: An independent reviewer applies the sovereignty rubric and records notes.
orchestration.stage4Desc: Operator publishes the listing and assigns a stable AIR-ID.
orchestration.stage5Desc: Consumers find the resource through the portal or the discovery API.
orchestration.stage6Desc: Provider keeps metadata accurate; status reflects changes over time.

faq.heading: Frequently asked questions
faq.faq1Q: Does the registry host any AI?
faq.faq1A: No. The registry only points. Providers operate their own resources, and hosting environments run the workloads. The registry is never on the runtime path.
faq.faq2A: A submission must cite at least one of: local law, local data, local systems, or local language and culture - with concrete evidence such as a referenced statute, dataset, or institutional integration.
faq.faq3A: Provider verification confirms that the listing is bound to the rightful operator via DNS and email proofs. It does not imply endorsement of the resource itself.
faq.faq4A: Any organisation or accredited individual that operates a sovereign AI resource can submit. Government endorsement is a separate, stronger signal granted only by the responsible authority.
faq.faq5A: Yes. The reference implementation at {portalDomain} and the AIR-SPEC are openly licensed. Each jurisdiction operates its own instance with local governance.
faq.faq6Q: How are listings resolved at runtime?
faq.faq6A: AIR-IDs (under air://) resolve to provider endpoints described in the listing metadata. Optionally, hosting environments issue SPIFFE/SPIRE SVIDs for runtime identity.
